nodes.label: Autonomous ingestion
nodes.description: When a user submits a project name + folder path via the React UI, the FastAPI backend spawns a dedicated claude --print --dangerously-skip-permissions subprocess (daemon thread, non-blocking). The subprocess receives INGEST_PROMPT_TEMPLATE which: discovers ALL relevant file types (*.md, *.py, *.jsx, *.tsx, *.js, *.sh — not just markdown), groups them into themed batches (docs, lib_pipeline, cli, ui_backend, ui_frontend, hooks), extracts structural decisions from each file (design choices, rationale, tradeoffs, known limits — not line-by-line logic), saves JSON payloads to projects/<name>/, runs brain.py ingest per batch, verifies with stats + audit, then runs gap verification per batch. Goal: after ingestion, the graph must be self-sufficient — the project must be fully reconstructable from the graph alone without reading source files. Output is streamed line-by-line into _jobs[job_id][output] and polled by the frontend every 2 seconds.

nodes.label: Code file extraction
nodes.description: Extraction of structural decisions from source code files (.py, .jsx, .js, .sh) as opposed to documentation files (.md). The protocol asks: why does this module/function exist? What does it prevent or enable? What alternative was rejected and why? What are its known limits? Explicitly excludes: variable names, line-by-line logic, boilerplate, imports. Explicitly includes: module purpose, design pattern chosen, security rationale, concurrency strategy, data structure choice and rationale, API contract and constraints. Enables the graph to be self-sufficient without retaining source files.

nodes.label: Graph self-sufficiency
nodes.description: Design invariant for brAIn: after a complete ingestion, the graph must contain enough information to fully reconstruct the project without reading any source file. This means every significant architectural decision, design tradeoff, security rationale, module purpose, and known limitation must be captured as nodes with causal edges. The source documents become disposable. This is the correctness criterion for extraction quality — more demanding than just capturing the main ideas.

nodes.label: Progressive graph disclosure
nodes.description: UI design pattern for the graph explorer: the canvas opens with only the single highest-importance node visible (brain_project, importance 1.0). The user expands the graph progressively by double-clicking any node, which reveals its 1-hop neighbors. Search and type-filter override this default and show their full result set. A Reset view button collapses back to the root node. Rationale: a 221-node graph rendered in full on load is visually overwhelming — the root node is the natural entry point into the knowledge structure. Implemented in App.jsx by seeding visibleNodeIds with only allNodes[0] when no search is active.

rels.evidence: The INGEST_PROMPT_TEMPLATE explicitly includes .py, .jsx, .js, .sh in the file discovery step and provides a separate extraction protocol for code files, enabling code-level decisions to be captured alongside documentation.

Documentation alone cannot make the graph self-sufficient — the design decisions that live only in code (module architecture, security patterns, concurrency choices) must also be extracted. Code file extraction closes this gap.
The autonomous ingestion pipeline, when run on a project with the updated INGEST_PROMPT_TEMPLATE covering all file types, produces a graph dense enough to satisfy the self-sufficiency invariant.
Progressive graph disclosure is the default view behavior of the React graph canvas — implemented in App.jsx by seeding visibleNodeIds with only allNodes[0] when no search is active.

Forcing users to start from the root node and expand deliberately mirrors the on-demand retrieval philosophy: only load what you need, when you need it — applied to human navigation as well as Claude's tool calls.
